infobox.value: This article **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Monaural_sound "Special:EditPage/Monaural sound") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *Find sources:* ["Monaural sound"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Monaural+sound%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Monaural+sound%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Monaural+sound%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Monaural+sound%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Monaural+sound%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Monaural+sound%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(September 2023)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))*

[newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Monaural+sound%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Monaural+sound%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Monaural+sound%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Monaural+sound%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(September 2023)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

or **monophonic sound** (often shortened to **mono**) is sound intended to be heard as if it were emanating from one position.[[1]](#cite_note-1) This contrasts with [stereophonic sound](/wiki/Stereophonic_sound "Stereophonic sound") or *stereo*, which uses two separate audio channels to reproduce sound from two microphones on the right and left side, which is reproduced with two separate [loudspeakers](/wiki/Loudspeaker "Loudspeaker") to give a sense of the direction of sound sources. In mono, only one [loudspeaker](/wiki/Loudspeaker "Loudspeaker") is necessary, but, when played through multiple loudspeakers or headphones, identical [audio signals](/wiki/Audio_signal "Audio signal") are fed to each speaker, resulting in the perception of one-channel sound "imaging" in one sonic space between the speakers (provided that the speakers are set up in a proper symmetrical critical-listening placement). Monaural recordings, like stereo ones, typically use multiple microphones fed into multiple channels on a recording console, but each channel is "[panned](/wiki/Panning_(audio) "Panning (audio)")" to the center. In the final stage, the various center-panned signal paths are usually [mixed down](/wiki/Audio_mixing_(recorded_music) "Audio mixing (recorded music)") to two identical tracks, which, because they are identical, are perceived upon playback as representing a single unified signal at a single place in the soundstage. In some cases, multitrack sources are mixed to a one-track tape, thus becoming one signal. In the [mastering](/wiki/Audio_mastering "Audio mastering") stage, particularly in the days of mono [records](/wiki/Phonograph_record "Phonograph record"), the one- or two-track mono master tape was then transferred to a one-track [lathe](/wiki/Disc_cutting_lathe "Disc cutting lathe") used to produce a master disc intended to be used in the pressing of a monophonic record. Today, however, monaural recordings are usually mastered to be played on stereo and multi-track formats, yet retain their center-panned mono soundstage characteristics.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Monaural](/w/index.php?title=Monaural&redirect=no "Monaural"))

Sound intended to be heard as if it were emanating from one position

"Monophonic" redirects here. For musical term, see [monophony](/wiki/Monophony "Monophony").

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This article **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Monaural_sound "Special:EditPage/Monaural sound") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *Find sources:* ["Monaural sound"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Monaural+sound%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Monaural+sound%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Monaural+sound%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Monaural+sound%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Monaural+sound%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Monaural+sound%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(September 2023)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/1_0_channels_%28mono%29_label.svg/120px-1_0_channels_%28mono%29_label.svg.png)](/wiki/File:1_0_channels_(mono)_label.svg)

A diagram of monaural sound

**Monaural sound** or **monophonic sound** (often shortened to **mono**) is sound intended to be heard as if it were emanating from one position.[[1]](#cite_note-1) This contrasts with [stereophonic sound](/wiki/Stereophonic_sound "Stereophonic sound") or *stereo*, which uses two separate audio channels to reproduce sound from two microphones on the right and left side, which is reproduced with two separate [loudspeakers](/wiki/Loudspeaker "Loudspeaker") to give a sense of the direction of sound sources. In mono, only one [loudspeaker](/wiki/Loudspeaker "Loudspeaker") is necessary, but, when played through multiple loudspeakers or headphones, identical [audio signals](/wiki/Audio_signal "Audio signal") are fed to each speaker, resulting in the perception of one-channel sound "imaging" in one sonic space between the speakers (provided that the speakers are set up in a proper symmetrical critical-listening placement). Monaural recordings, like stereo ones, typically use multiple microphones fed into multiple channels on a recording console, but each channel is "[panned](/wiki/Panning_(audio) "Panning (audio)")" to the center. In the final stage, the various center-panned signal paths are usually [mixed down](/wiki/Audio_mixing_(recorded_music) "Audio mixing (recorded music)") to two identical tracks, which, because they are identical, are perceived upon playback as representing a single unified signal at a single place in the soundstage. In some cases, multitrack sources are mixed to a one-track tape, thus becoming one signal. In the [mastering](/wiki/Audio_mastering "Audio mastering") stage, particularly in the days of mono [records](/wiki/Phonograph_record "Phonograph record"), the one- or two-track mono master tape was then transferred to a one-track [lathe](/wiki/Disc_cutting_lathe "Disc cutting lathe") used to produce a master disc intended to be used in the pressing of a monophonic record. Today, however, monaural recordings are usually mastered to be played on stereo and multi-track formats, yet retain their center-panned mono soundstage characteristics.

Monaural sound has largely been replaced by [stereo](/wiki/Stereophonic_sound "Stereophonic sound") sound in most entertainment applications, but remains the standard for [radiotelephone](/wiki/Radiotelephone "Radiotelephone") communications, [telephone](/wiki/Telephone "Telephone") networks, and [audio induction loops](/wiki/Audio_induction_loop "Audio induction loop") for use with [hearing aids](/wiki/Hearing_aid "Hearing aid").[[2]](#cite_note-2) [FM radio](/wiki/FM_broadcasting "FM broadcasting") stations broadcast in stereo, while most [AM radio](/wiki/AM_radio "AM radio") stations broadcast in mono. (Although an [AM stereo](/wiki/AM_stereo "AM stereo") broadcast standard exists, few AM stations are equipped to use it.) A few FM stations—notably [talk-radio](/wiki/Talk_radio "Talk radio") stations—choose to broadcast in monaural because of the slight advantage in [signal strength](/wiki/Signal_strength_in_telecommunications "Signal strength in telecommunications") and [bandwidth](/wiki/Bandwidth_(signal_processing) "Bandwidth (signal processing)") the standard affords over a stereophonic signal of the same power.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monaural_sound&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

While some experiments were made with stereophonic recording and reproduction from the early days of the [phonograph](/wiki/Phonograph "Phonograph") in the late-19th century, monaural was the rule for almost all [audio recording](/wiki/Sound_recording_and_reproduction "Sound recording and reproduction") until the second half of the 20th century.

Monaural sound is normal on:

* [Phonograph cylinders](/wiki/Phonograph_cylinder "Phonograph cylinder")
* [Gramophone records](/wiki/Phonograph_record "Phonograph record") made before 1958, such as those made for playing at 78 [rpm](/wiki/Revolutions_per_minute "Revolutions per minute") and earlier 16+2⁄3, 33+1⁄3 and 45 rpm microgroove records
* [AM broadcasting](/wiki/AM_broadcasting "AM broadcasting")
* Some [FM radio](/wiki/FM_broadcasting "FM broadcasting") stations that broadcast only spoken-word or [talk-radio](/wiki/Talk_radio "Talk radio") content (in order to maximize their coverage area)
* [Subcarrier](/wiki/Subcarrier "Subcarrier") signals for FM radio, which carry leased content such as [background music](/wiki/Background_music "Background music") for businesses or a [radio reading service](/wiki/Radio_reading_service "Radio reading service")
* Background music services such as [Seeburg 1000](/wiki/Seeburg_1000 "Seeburg 1000"); satellite broadcasts by [Muzak](/wiki/Muzak_(brand) "Muzak (brand)"), and some [public address systems](/wiki/Public_address_system "Public address system")

Incompatible standards exist for:

* Later vinyl records (although monophonic records—which had almost disappeared in the United States by the end of 1967—could be played with a stereo [cartridge](/wiki/Magnetic_cartridge "Magnetic cartridge"))
* [Reel-to-reel audio tape recording](/wiki/Reel-to-reel_audio_tape_recording "Reel-to-reel audio tape recording") (depending on track alignment)

Compatible monaural and stereophonic standards exist for:

* [MiniDisc](/wiki/MiniDisc "MiniDisc")
* [Compact audio cassette](/wiki/Compact_Cassette "Compact Cassette")
* [FM](/wiki/FM_broadcasting "FM broadcasting") (and, in rare circumstances, [AM](/wiki/AM_broadcasting "AM broadcasting")) radio broadcasting
* [VCR](/wiki/Videocassette_recorder "Videocassette recorder") formats
* [TV](/wiki/Television "Television")
* [Digital audio files](/wiki/Digital_audio_file "Digital audio file") on many computers in many formats ([WAV](/wiki/WAV "WAV"), [MP3](/wiki/MP3 "MP3"), etc.)

No native monaural standards exist for:

* [8-track tape](/wiki/8-track_tape "8-track tape")
* [Compact disc](/wiki/Compact_disc "Compact disc")

In those formats, the mono-source material is presented as two identical channels, thus being technically stereo.

At various times artists have preferred to work in mono, either in recognition of the technical limitations of the equipment of the era or because of simple preference (this can be seen as analogous to filmmakers working in [black and white](/wiki/Black-and-white "Black-and-white")). An example is [John Mellencamp](/wiki/John_Mellencamp "John Mellencamp")'s 2010 album *[No Better Than This](/wiki/No_Better_Than_This "No Better Than This")*, recorded in mono to emulate mid-20th century blues and folk records. Some early recordings such as [The Beatles](/wiki/The_Beatles "The Beatles")' first four albums (*[Please Please Me](/wiki/Please_Please_Me "Please Please Me")*, *[With the Beatles](/wiki/With_the_Beatles "With the Beatles")*, *[A Hard Day's Night](/wiki/A_Hard_Day%27s_Night_(album) "A Hard Day's Night (album)")*, *[Beatles for Sale](/wiki/Beatles_for_Sale "Beatles for Sale")*) were re-released in the [CD](/wiki/Compact_disc "Compact disc") era as monophonic in recognition of the fact that the source tapes for the earliest recordings were two-track, with vocals on one track and instruments on the other (even though this was only true on the first two albums, while the latter two had been recorded on four-track). This was actually intended to provide flexibility in producing a final mono mix, not to provide a stereo recording, although because of demand this was done anyway, and the early material was available on vinyl in both mono and stereo formats. In the 1960s and 1970s, it was common in the pop world for stereophonic versions of mono tracks to be generated electronically using filtering techniques to attempt to pick out various instruments and vocals; but these were often considered unsatisfactory, owing to the artifacts of the conversion process.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Directors [Stanley Kubrick](/wiki/Stanley_Kubrick "Stanley Kubrick") and [Woody Allen](/wiki/Woody_Allen "Woody Allen"), among others, preferred to record their films' [sound tracks](/wiki/Soundtrack "Soundtrack") in mono.

Monaural LP records were eventually phased out and no longer manufactured after the early 1970s, with a few exceptions. For example, Decca UK had a few double issues until the end of 1970 – the last one being Tom Jones's "I Who Have Nothing"; in [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil") records were released in both mono and stereo as late as 1972.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] During the 1960s it was common for albums to be released as both mono and stereo LPs, occasionally with slight differences between the two (again, detailed information of The Beatles' recordings provides a good example of the differences). This was because many people owned mono record players that were incapable of playing stereo records, as well as the prevalence of AM radio. Because of the limited quantities pressed and alternative mixes of several tracks, the monaural versions of these albums are often valued more highly than their stereo LP counterparts in record-collecting circles today.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

On 9 September 2009, The Beatles re-released a remastered box set of their mono output spanning the *[Please Please Me](/wiki/Please_Please_Me "Please Please Me")* album to *[The Beatles](/wiki/The_Beatles_(album) "The Beatles (album)")* (commonly called "The White Album"). The set, simply called *[The Beatles in Mono](/wiki/The_Beatles_in_Mono "The Beatles in Mono")*, also includes a two-disc summary of the mono singles, B-sides and EP tracks released throughout their career. Also included were five tracks originally mixed for an unissued mono *[Yellow Submarine](/wiki/Yellow_Submarine_(album) "Yellow Submarine (album)")* [EP](/wiki/Extended_play "Extended play"). [Bob Dylan](/wiki/Bob_Dylan "Bob Dylan") followed suit on 19 October 2010 with *[The Original Mono Recordings](/wiki/The_Original_Mono_Recordings "The Original Mono Recordings")*, a box set featuring the mono releases from *[Bob Dylan](/wiki/Bob_Dylan_(album) "Bob Dylan (album)")* (1962) to *[John Wesley Harding](/wiki/John_Wesley_Harding "John Wesley Harding")* (1967). On 21 November 2011, [The Kinks](/wiki/The_Kinks "The Kinks")' mono recordings were issued as *[The Kinks in Mono](/wiki/The_Kinks_in_Mono "The Kinks in Mono")* box set, featuring the releases of the band's albums from *[Kinks](/wiki/Kinks_(album) "Kinks (album)")* (1964) to *[Arthur](/wiki/Arthur_(Or_the_Decline_and_Fall_of_the_British_Empire) "Arthur (Or the Decline and Fall of the British Empire)")* (1969), with three additional CDs of non-album tracks that appeared as singles or EP tracks. When the initial run of the box set sold out, no more were pressed, unlike the Beatles and Dylan sets.

## Compatibility between mono and stereo sound

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monaural_sound&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Compatibility between mono and stereo sound")]

Sometimes *mono sound* or *monaural* can simply refer to a merged pair of stereo channels - also known as "collapsed stereo" or "folded-down stereo". Over time some devices have used mono sound amplification circuitry with two or more speakers since it can cut the cost of the hardware. Some consumer electronics with stereo RCA outputs have a microswitch in the red RCA output (i.e., the right stereo channel) that disables merging of stereo sound into the white (left stereo channel) RCA output. Common devices with this are [VCRs](/wiki/VCR "VCR"), [DVD](/wiki/DVD_player "DVD player")/[Blu-ray players](/wiki/Blu-ray_player "Blu-ray player"), information appliances, [set-top boxes](/wiki/Set-top_box "Set-top box"), and the like. [Video game consoles](/wiki/Video_game_console "Video game console") sometimes have male RCA ends of cables with a proprietary multi-A/V plug on the other end, which prevents automatic stereo merging unless adapters are used.

Disadvantages of merged stereo involve phase cancellations that may have the effect of muffling the final sound output. If channels are merged after being sent through a power amplifier but before being connected to a loudspeaker, it places more stress on the [loudspeaker](/wiki/Loudspeaker "Loudspeaker"). It has usually been the practice in recording studios to make separate mixes for mono recordings (rather than folded-down stereo-to-mono), so that the final mono master will avoid the pitfalls of collapsed stereo. In video games, merging stereo to mono sound prevents player from discerning what direction distant SFX are coming from, and reverse stereo has a similar setback too. Having an array of loudspeakers connected to their own amplifier outputs can mitigate issues with the electrical load for a single loudspeaker coil and allow the listener to perceive an "image" of sound in the free space between the speakers.

### Mirrored mono

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monaural_sound&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Mirrored mono")]

Mirrored mono sound is the opposite of merged stereo, since it can be a case where media with mono sound that stereo playback devices automatically mirror it with are played on both channels of the receiver. It can also mean having a mono input mixed down to stereo amplification circuitry, or a mono system with a headphone output compatible with stereo [headphones](/wiki/Headphones "Headphones"). An example of an application where both merged stereo and mirror mono apply is when a [compact audio cassette](/wiki/Compact_cassette "Compact cassette") respectively plays back "summed" stereo channels on a mono reading head, and when a compact cassette recorded with mono sound is played back with a stereo tape head.

Other instances of "mirrored mono" also include using the right stereo channel in lieu of a "left" one (or vice versa) where both channels are wired to mirror only one.

### Both

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monaural_sound&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Both")]

Instances of both "merged stereo" and "mirrored mono" can occur when the stereo channels are merged to a mono system with stereo headphone compatibility or when a mono system has "twin speakers" (or "pseudo-stereo").

Other applications that involve mirrored mono with merged stereo occur when MONO is available as an internal feature of a device that can toggle between STEREO and MONO, for instance many [FM radios](/wiki/FM_radio "FM radio") are capable of toggling between MONO and STEREO in a way where stereo can both MERGE into mono, and then MIRROR between both stereo speakers. This tactic can also be used on other devices, of which computer software and some [video games](/wiki/Video_game "Video game") will have a feature that will allow STEREO or MONO for the soundtrack, in which sometimes this can facilitate MERGING stereo internally to spare one from using a Y adapter with LEFT and RIGHT RCA plugs when using mono equipment, such as [guitar amplifiers](/wiki/Guitar_amplifier "Guitar amplifier").

### Native stereo equipment with mono-only features

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monaural_sound&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Native stereo equipment with mono-only features")]

Some [TV/VCR combo](/wiki/TV/VCR_combo "TV/VCR combo") decks on the market had stereo TV functionality with "twin speakers", whereas the VCR feature was only mono, which is typical of "consumer-grade VCRs" from decades ago. Some of these devices even had front RCA inputs for composite video (yellow), and mono audio (white) in which many of these devices didn't even have a right-channel RCA plug (red) even if it was just for "merging" stereo into mono for mono soundtracks to be recorded onto videotapes. This is odd since one would think that a "right channel" would be included for A/V in on a TV which had MTS stereo TV sound on its tuner.

Some stereo receivers will also include mono microphone inputs.
